Observational Analysis

Anonymous

2025-02-17

This notebook contains the observational analysis of the impact of requirements quality (RQ) on trace link recovery (TLR).

Data Loading

First, load the data for the observational analysis. The data set is procured by the data-preparation.Rmd notebook. This notebook assembles and casts the data appropriately and stores the results in the rq4tlr-dtata.csv file.

df <- read.csv('./data/rq4tlr-data.csv')

head(df)
##   Dataset File duplicate nameproblem scope textorder iounquantified actoractor
## 1   etour  UC1     FALSE       FALSE FALSE     FALSE          FALSE      FALSE
## 2   etour UC10     FALSE       FALSE FALSE     FALSE          FALSE      FALSE
## 3   etour UC11     FALSE       FALSE FALSE     FALSE          FALSE      FALSE
## 4   etour UC12     FALSE       FALSE FALSE     FALSE          FALSE      FALSE
## 5   etour UC13     FALSE       FALSE FALSE     FALSE          FALSE      FALSE
## 6   etour UC14     FALSE       FALSE FALSE     FALSE          FALSE      FALSE
##   justification coordambiguity uidesign alternative inconsistentloa
## 1         FALSE           0.00        0           0               0
## 2         FALSE           0.00        0           0               0
## 3         FALSE           0.00        0           0               0
## 4         FALSE           0.00        0           0               0
## 5         FALSE           0.75        0           0               0
## 6         FALSE           0.00        0           0               0
##   clarification happy size meanactor meanuc coherence outlinks maxinlinks
## 1             0  TRUE    4         1  FALSE 0.0000000        4          5
## 2             0  TRUE    2         1  FALSE 0.0000000        7          7
## 3             0  TRUE    2         1  FALSE 1.0000000        2          3
## 4             0  TRUE    2         1  FALSE 0.0000000        4          7
## 5             0  TRUE    4         1  FALSE 0.3333333        5          7
## 6             0  TRUE    4         1  FALSE 0.3333333        5          7
##   anaphora optional nonnounstart incompcomparison passive negation
## 1     0.25        0         1.00                0       0        0
## 2     0.50        0         1.00                0       0        0
## 3     0.00        0         1.00                0       0        0
## 4     0.00        0         0.50                0       0        0
## 5     0.25        0         0.75                0       0        0
## 6     0.00        0         1.00                0       0        0
##   maxsteplength sencomplexity lostfr tp fp fn Precision Recall        F1
## 1            23             7   TRUE  4 16  0 0.2000000   1.00 0.3333333
## 2            27             8  FALSE  7 20  0 0.2592593   1.00 0.4117647
## 3            10             3  FALSE  2  9  0 0.1818182   1.00 0.3076923
## 4            22             6  FALSE  3 14  1 0.1764706   0.75 0.2857143
## 5            14             4  FALSE  4 12  1 0.2500000   0.80 0.3809524
## 6             8             2  FALSE  5 17  0 0.2272727   1.00 0.3703704
##          AP      LAG
## 1 0.2941850  6.25000
## 2 0.2673259 10.14286
## 3 0.2111111  5.50000
## 4 0.1417626 14.25000
## 5 0.2304762 10.20000
## 6 0.1840100 12.60000

Analysis

The analysis of the data employs a regression model in a Bayesian framework.

Distribution

The three outcome variables Precision, Recall, and F1 are all bounded \(\in [0, 1]\). This makes a Beta distribution an eligible target distribution of the response variables. To determine which variant of the Beta distribution family can be chosen, we visualize the raw distribution.

df %>% 
  select(c(Precision, Recall, F1)) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols=c(Precision, Recall, F1), names_to = "Metric") %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = value, fill=Metric)) +
    geom_histogram()
## `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value with `binwidth`.

A base Beta distribution would require that all values are bounded within \(]0; 1[\). The observed values for the three outcome variables show a clear zero- and one-inflation. Hence, we chose the zero_one_inflated_beta distribution from the brms library.

Formula

Next, we define regression formulae that put the outcome variable of interest in relation to the predictors with which we assume a causal relationship.

f1 <- F1 ~ 0 + Intercept + Dataset + sencomplexity + maxsteplength + size + inconsistentloa + uidesign + clarification + alternative + coherence + happy + lostfr + anaphora + nonnounstart + passive + negation + outlinks + maxinlinks
#f2 <- bf(
#  mvbind(Precision, Recall, F1) ~ 0 + Intercept + Dataset + sencomplexity + maxsteplength + size + inconsistentloa + uidesign + happy,
#  family = zero_one_inflated_beta
#)

The 0 + Intercept term instead of 1 + or even complete omission stems from the fact that the regression formula contains at least one predictor that is not centered around 0.

Priors

The selected regression formula requires the definition of prior distributions for the following parameters.

get_prior(f1, data=df, family=zero_one_inflated_beta)
##              prior class            coef group resp dpar nlpar lb ub
##             (flat)     b                                            
##             (flat)     b     alternative                            
##             (flat)     b        anaphora                            
##             (flat)     b   clarification                            
##             (flat)     b       coherence                            
##             (flat)     b   Datasetitrust                            
##             (flat)     b       happyTRUE                            
##             (flat)     b inconsistentloa                            
##             (flat)     b       Intercept                            
##             (flat)     b      lostfrTRUE                            
##             (flat)     b      maxinlinks                            
##             (flat)     b   maxsteplength                            
##             (flat)     b        negation                            
##             (flat)     b    nonnounstart                            
##             (flat)     b        outlinks                            
##             (flat)     b         passive                            
##             (flat)     b   sencomplexity                            
##             (flat)     b            size                            
##             (flat)     b        uidesign                            
##         beta(1, 1)   coi                                        0  1
##  gamma(0.01, 0.01)   phi                                        0   
##         beta(1, 1)   zoi                                        0  1
##        source
##       default
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##  (vectorized)
##       default
##       default
##       default

We select the following priors:

  • \(N(0, 1)\) for the intercept, to allow a flexible baseline
  • \(N(0, 0.5)\) centered, uninformative priors for all factors
  • \(Gam(4, 1)\) for the concentration parameter \(\phi\)
  • \(Beta(7, 2)\) for the zero-inflation
  • \(Beta(4, 2)\) for the one-inflation
p1 <- c(
  prior(normal(0, 1), class = b, coef = "Intercept"),
  prior(normal(0, 0.5), class = b),
  prior(gamma(4, 1), class = phi),
  prior(beta(7, 2), class = coi), 
  prior(beta(5, 2), class = zoi) 
)

Prior Predictive Check

The prior predictive check helps determine the eligibility of the priors.

m1.prior <-
  brm(data = df, family = zero_one_inflated_beta, f1, prior = p1,
    iter = 4000, warmup = 1000, chains = 4, cores = 4,
    seed = 4, sample_prior="only",
    file = "fits/obs.f1.prior"
  )

In our particular case, the large number of predictors will, however, push the accumulated value of \(\mu\) in the Beta-distribution to the to extremes.

brms::pp_check(m1.prior, ndraws=100)

The figure shows extreme concentrations of expected values \(y_{rep}\) on the borders of the output space (at 0 and 1). Instead, we put more emphasis on the posterior predictive check.

Training

Assuming the priors are reliable, we train the model by updating the coefficient parameters with the data df.

m1 <-
  brm(data = df, family = zero_one_inflated_beta, f1, prior = p1,
    iter = 4000, warmup = 1000, chains = 4, cores = 4, seed = 4,
    file = "fits/obs.f1"
  )

To check whether the training completed successfully, we inspect the posterior predictive check.

brms::pp_check(m1, ndraws=100)

The output confirms that the model parameters have been updated properly and that the predicted outcomes \(y_{rep}\) are close the the actual outcome \(y\).

Evaluation

Finally, we evaluate the model.

Coefficients

First, we look at the updated path coefficients in the model summary.

summary(m1)
##  Family: zero_one_inflated_beta 
##   Links: mu = logit; phi = identity; zoi = identity; coi = identity 
## Formula: F1 ~ 0 + Intercept + Dataset + sencomplexity + maxsteplength + size + inconsistentloa + uidesign + clarification + alternative + coherence + happy + lostfr + anaphora + nonnounstart + passive + negation + outlinks + maxinlinks 
##    Data: df (Number of observations: 189) 
##   Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 4000; warmup = 1000; thin = 1;
##          total post-warmup draws = 12000
## 
## Population-Level Effects: 
##                 Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
## Intercept          -0.63      0.33    -1.28     0.02 1.00     9929     8586
## Datasetitrust       0.05      0.26    -0.47     0.56 1.00    10602     8966
## sencomplexity      -0.01      0.04    -0.09     0.08 1.00    10439     7788
## maxsteplength      -0.00      0.01    -0.02     0.02 1.00    10636     8027
## size               -0.00      0.01    -0.02     0.01 1.00    14348    10193
## inconsistentloa     0.47      0.30    -0.13     1.06 1.00    16495     8761
## uidesign           -0.07      0.47    -0.99     0.85 1.00    16173     8933
## clarification      -0.15      0.29    -0.73     0.41 1.00    17798     9071
## alternative        -0.31      0.38    -1.05     0.45 1.00    16692     9389
## coherence          -0.06      0.21    -0.47     0.34 1.00    12966     8499
## happyTRUE           0.11      0.20    -0.27     0.49 1.00    11634     9363
## lostfrTRUE          0.07      0.28    -0.48     0.62 1.00    16752     8579
## anaphora           -0.25      0.25    -0.74     0.24 1.00    15666     9213
## nonnounstart       -0.28      0.23    -0.74     0.18 1.00    11173     8701
## passive            -0.08      0.28    -0.63     0.48 1.00    13495     9585
## negation            0.34      0.37    -0.40     1.05 1.00    16712     8589
## outlinks            0.12      0.04     0.05     0.19 1.00    13485     9440
## maxinlinks         -0.02      0.01    -0.03     0.00 1.00    14923     8841
## 
## Family Specific Parameters: 
##     Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
## phi     9.30      1.23     7.05    11.84 1.00    14311     9006
## zoi     0.44      0.04     0.37     0.51 1.00    18850     8132
## coi     0.13      0.04     0.07     0.21 1.00    16358     7395
## 
## Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS
## and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential
## scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).

These coefficients can also be visualized via a mcmc_plot.

mcmc_plot(m1, variable=c("b_Datasetitrust", "b_sencomplexity", "b_maxsteplength", "b_size", "b_inconsistentloa", "b_uidesign", "b_clarification", "b_alternative", "b_coherence", "b_happyTRUE", "b_lostfrTRUE", "b_anaphora", "b_nonnounstart", "b_passive", "b_negation", "b_outlinks", "b_maxinlinks"))

Variables, where the 50% confidence interval of the intercept does not overlap 0, weakly impact the response variable. Variables, where the 95% confidence interval of the intercept does not overlap 0, strongly impact the response variable.

Marginal Distributions

For a clearer picture, we can plot the marginal distributions of the outcome variable when manipulating one specific factor while keeping the others at a constant level.

conditional_effects(m1, effects="inconsistentloa", method="posterior_epred")

conditional_effects(m1, effects="alternative", method="posterior_epred")

conditional_effects(m1, effects="anaphora", method="posterior_epred")

conditional_effects(m1, effects="nonnounstart", method="posterior_epred")

conditional_effects(m1, effects="negation", method="posterior_epred")

conditional_effects(m1, effects="outlinks", method="posterior_epred")
